title: Poems From "A Shropshire Lad" - LII
content:
Far in a western brookland
    That bred me long ago
    The poplars stand and tremble
    By pools I used to know.

    There, in the windless night-time,
    The wanderer, marvelling why,
    Halts on the bridge to hearken
    How soft the poplars sigh.

    He hears: long since forgotten
    In fields where I was known,
    Here I lie down in London
    And turn to rest alone.

    There, by the starlit fences,
    The wanderer halts and hears
    My soul that lingers sighing
    About the glimmering weirs.
meaning:
This evocative piece by Alfred Edward Housman, titled "Poems From "A Shropshire Lad" - LII", represents a masterful exploration of classic. The lines capture a profound emotional resonance...

### Why We Love This Line
At Linespedia, we believe that poetry is the ultimate sanctuary for the soul...
